title: Kit-side context and vault-side skills must not cross over
body: This repo carries two scopes at once: kit-development context (AGENTS.md, docs/, llm_wiki_kit/) which agents working on the kit read, and vault-side skills (core/files/skills/**, templates/*/files/skills/**) which get copied into a user's vault and read by their Claude session. Never let kit-development context bleed into a vault-side SKILL.md, and never write kit code that assumes a vault path other than what's explicitly passed in. See AGENTS.md § Two scopes, one repo.

title: Never bypass write_helper.safe_write() for vault writes
body: Any kit code that writes into a user's vault must route through write_helper.safe_write(). Drift detection is load-bearing — raw open()/Path.write_text() calls escape the journal and break what wiki doctor relies on for reconciliation. If you find yourself reaching for a plain write, stop and route through the helper. See AGENTS.md § Things you should not do without asking.

title: Commit format: v2 tasks now, conventional commits later
body: During v2 development every commit corresponds to a numbered task in docs/rfc/0001-v2-architecture.md and uses the form `v2: task <N> - <one-line summary>`. After v2 ships, the repo switches to conventional commits (`feat:`, `fix:`, `docs:`, `refactor:`, `test:`, `chore:`) with the affected module in parens when useful, e.g. `fix(journal): handle empty file as zero events`. See docs/CONVENTIONS.md § Commit messages.

title: Don't hand-edit CHARTER.md substantively without an RFC
body: The charter is frozen by design: substantive changes go through an RFC so scope shifts get reviewed and recorded. Trivial fixes (typos, broken links, clarifications) are fine as a normal PR, but rewriting goals, scope, or non-goals directly skips the discussion that keeps the project honest about what it is and isn't. See AGENTS.md § Things you should not do without asking and docs/CONVENTIONS.md § The doc hierarchy.

title: Test pure functions directly, keep one test per surface as the wiring check
body: When a doctor check (or any pure function) is reachable via a top-level entry point (`run_doctor`, `cli.main`, etc.), the temptation is to test it only via that entry point so the test is 'end-to-end'. Quality-engineer review in PR #30 flagged this as the wrong default: route-through tests pay full vault-setup cost for every branch, and they mask layering bugs (e.g. a future refactor that has the function re-read env vars itself would still pass). Default: write direct unit tests for each branch of the pure function with synthetic state, then keep ONE end-to-end test per surface as the wiring check. The integration test in `tests/integration/` covers the full path.

title: Spec drift fixed in the same PR — take the reviewer's signature, amend the contract
body: When implementation review surfaces a cleaner signature than the spec/plan describes (PR #30: `check_stale_lock(events, vault_root, threshold_hours)` → `(state, threshold_hours)` once `state.held_lock` made the events walk redundant), do the refactor AND amend spec.md + plan.md in the same PR. AGENTS.md's 'drift is a bug' rule is the controlling principle: a spec that names a signature the code doesn't expose lies to the next reader. The cost is a few spec lines; the alternative is the spec slowly diverging until nobody reads it. Same applies to §Risks resolutions adopted late (the plan named a `stop_on_corruption=True` flag but §Risks recommended `read_events_lenient`; both shipped and got amended together).

title: When adding a non-blocking flock mode, plumb it through every flock site
body: PR #32 (step 5) initially added `nonblocking=True` only to `transaction()`. Adversarial review caught that the stale-holder reclaim path also calls `append_event` BEFORE the transaction — and `append_event`'s own flock was still blocking. A competitor that won between the probe and the reclaim-append would hang the CLI on the reclaim's blocking flock, defeating the whole non-blocking design. Fix: `append_event(..., nonblocking=True)` plus a `try/except LockUnavailableError` arm. Pattern: any function-level retry-by-blocking call that the CLI invokes in a non-blocking flow needs its own `nonblocking` parameter — surveying every flock site is part of the change, not a follow-up.

title: Event-before-disk ordering, with two documented bypasses
body: Every kit-to-vault write in write_helper.py appends its journal event before opening the target for write — so a crash between the two leaves a recoverable state (wiki doctor's missing / page-drift / managed-region-drift checks reconcile the gap; no rollback). Two documented bypasses: (1) resolve_proposal still appends events before the disk write but bypasses the drift check, because conflict resolution is the user-mediated acknowledgement that overwrite is intended; (2) _ensure_obsidianignore writes .obsidianignore without journaling, because journaling would register every user edit as page-drift in wiki doctor (the file is user-editable by design) — see OBSIDIANIGNORE_BYPASS_DOC. Gotcha: adding a third bypass requires amending docs/specs/safe-write-ordering/spec.md; the journaling story is load-bearing. Parent entry: K-0002 (forbids bypasses categorically); this entry refines K-0002 with the two sanctioned exceptions.

title: ContextVar-as-cache-handle, with writer-side notification
body: Per-invocation caches that survive across many call sites without threading a kwarg through every signature should use a ContextVar + a context-manager scope (use_journal_cache mirrors journal.transaction's _HELD_FD pattern). Cache invalidation is done by the writer side: append_event calls notify_appended(event) on the matching active reader after fsync returns, so the cache stays equal to disk for the rest of the scope without mtime polling (mtime resolution varies by filesystem; in-process notification is exact). Gotcha: every append site has to fire the notify, including held-fd branches inside transaction() — surveying every append site is part of the change, not a follow-up (mirrors K-0007's flock-everywhere lesson). Lazy-load contract: notify_appended is a no-op until events() has been called once, to avoid conjuring in-memory state for events never observed by reading. Routing fallthrough calls in consumer modules (write_helper._read_events_cached) through journal.read_events (not the import-time binding) preserves monkeypatch-visibility for tests.

title: Reuse install.aggregate_region_contributions over generalising install_primitives
body: wiki upgrade re-applies installed primitives against the running kit's catalog. The temptation was to generalise install_primitives so it could emit either PrimitiveInstallEvent or PrimitiveUpgradeEvent via a callback. Declined: the runner shape is 'one PrimitiveUpgradeEvent per upgraded primitive, then reuse render_tree + aggregate_region_contributions exactly as they stand' — a separate runner module (upgrade.py) is shorter than a callback parameter on a shared installer and keeps install_primitives' contract narrow. Pattern: when two pipelines differ only in their event taxonomy, write a sibling runner that reuses the shared pure helpers (validate_contributions, aggregate_region_contributions, render_tree, safe_write) rather than parameterising the existing pipeline. Idempotency at the empty-to_upgrade case is a CLI-level short-circuit, not a runner-level invariant — safe_write_region appends a ManagedRegionWriteEvent per bucket even on hash-match, so 'no work' had to be a decision the caller makes before entering the runner. Gotcha: post-run sidecar collection by journal-tail slice must use the disk-re-reading journal.read_events (not the cached JournalReader.events()), because slicing the cache's internal list aliases mutable state.

title: Derived event ids beat content-hashed event ids when subprocesses and Pydantic-version drift are in play
body: wiki-run-exec needed a way to reference a dispatch event from a later failure event AND name on-disk artifacts (inbox/scheduled-failures/<id>.md, .wiki.journal/exec-logs/<id>.log) after it. First instinct: hash the dispatch event's canonical JSON. Adversarial review caught three problems: (1) Pydantic v2's dump_json doesn't sort dict keys — `args={a,b}` and `args={b,a}` hash to different ids for byte-identical-on-write events; (2) Pydantic minor versions occasionally change emission shape (timezone suffix, null-emission), so a recomputed id can stop matching an on-disk filename after a kit upgrade; (3) two scheduled --exec calls landing in the same microsecond produce identical events → identical hashes → log file truncation race. Switched to `uuid.uuid4().hex[:12]` generated at dispatch time and stored as an additive `event_id: str | None = None` on OperationRunEvent (default None makes old journal lines replay clean per ADR-0002). DispatchResult.dispatch_event_id carries the same string back to callers. No content-derivation anywhere. Cost: 12-char filename is opaque; benefit: cross-process unique, Pydantic-version-stable, future-proof.

title: Don't reuse `now` for dispatched_at AND failed_at when the failure file shows duration
body: wiki-run-exec orchestrator originally passed the same `now` timestamp for both the dispatch event AND the failure event, then `_render_failure_file` computed `duration = failed_at - dispatched_at = 0s`. The docstring even acknowledged the drift ('Real failure-event timestamps in production would drift slightly... the journal field carries the original now from this argument so tests stay deterministic'). Quality review caught it: 'a 30-min timeout renders as duration 0s in the per-failure file, which is the field operators read to triage.' Fix: add a `failure_clock: Callable[[], datetime] | None` kwarg defaulting to `_utc_now`; tests inject `lambda: now + timedelta(seconds=K)` for determinism. Pattern: when a duration or wall-clock-elapsed value lands in a user-visible artifact, the producer must take TWO timestamps (or a clock callable), not one. Test-determinism is a fine reason to take a clock parameter; it's not a reason to render a wrong value in production.

title: Conductor sibling workspaces share one Python env — re-pin the editable install after switching workspace
body: Multiple Conductor workspaces (tianjin, albuquerque, ...) share one site-packages. `pip show llm-wiki-kit | grep 'Editable project location'` shows which sibling the pointer aims at. Symptom: `python -m llm_wiki_kit run --exec ...` reports 'unrecognized arguments: --exec' even though the subparser definitely registers it — because the running interpreter is loading code from a stale sibling that doesn't have the change. Fix: `pip install -e '.[dev]'` from the current workspace. Already in MEMORY.md as a feedback entry; promoted here because hitting it on cli.py changes is high-cost: argparse 'unrecognized arguments' looks like a code bug, not an install pointer bug, and the failure mode looks like a real subparser issue.

title: Ruff RUF002/RUF003 reject ambiguous unicode in docstrings and comments
body: Ruff's ambiguous-character rules fire on em-dash (—), en-dash (–), the union symbol (∪), and other look-alike unicode in docstrings (RUF002) and comments (RUF003) — even when the surrounding code is correct. Symptoms: lint fails on prose like `rules 1–4` or `subcommands ∪ aliases` in a docstring even though the test suite is green. Use ASCII hyphens (`-`), and spell out set operations in prose (`union of A and B`). Comments inside frozensets that use a Unicode arrow (e.g. `cli.py → primitives.py`) also trip RUF003; replace with `cli.py -> primitives.py`.
